title: The hidden cost of context engineering
description: The biggest risk in using AI for software engineering may be to engineering minds themselves.
contentMarkdown:
One of the big promises of AI is that it lets you work at a higher level.

Instead of doing everything yourself, you define the task, provide the right context, set the constraints, and let the model execute. In theory, this is pure leverage. You spend less time in the weeds and more time directing work.

And to be fair, that promise is real. Context engineering is useful. It makes delegation easier, reduces blank-page friction, and can dramatically increase output.

But it has a hidden cost.

If you are not careful, context engineering turns your day into a sequence of small handoffs, reviews, corrections, and re-briefs. You stop doing every task yourself, but you still have to mentally load each one. The result is that you save execution time while quietly increasing context switching.

That matters more than most people admit, especially in software engineering.

A lot of engineering work is abstract before it becomes concrete. Before you write code, you often have to rebuild the problem in your head: what the system is doing, what assumptions it depends on, where the edge cases are, what “done” should mean, and which tradeoffs matter here. Getting into that state takes time. In practice, it often feels like it takes 10 to 30 minutes before your brain is fully inside a task. Not because you are lazy or distracted, but because deep work usually has a warm-up cost.

Research broadly supports this intuition. Gloria Mark’s work on attention and interruptions has shown that knowledge workers operate in highly fragmented environments, and that switching tasks carries measurable cognitive costs and attention residue rather than a clean reset from one item to the next.[^1] Sophie Leroy’s research on attention residue makes a similar point: when you switch tasks, part of your attention stays attached to the previous one, which reduces performance on the next.[^2] Even if the exact number varies by person and task, the underlying truth is obvious to anyone who has tried to do real engineering work. Focus is not instant. It has to be rebuilt.

This is where context engineering gets tricky.

It sounds like a focus aid because you are no longer manually executing every step. But in practice, many AI workflows ask you to jump between multiple partially loaded problems all day long. You frame one task for an agent. Then you review another output. Then you fix a misunderstanding in a third thread. Then you remember one missing constraint in the first task. Then you switch into product mode for a spec. Then technical mode for a bug. Then editorial mode for rewriting. Then reviewer mode for cleanup.

That can feel like momentum because many things are moving at once. But movement is not the same thing as depth.

The dangerous part is that AI-driven context switching disguises itself as leverage. It does not feel like ordinary distraction. It feels productive. You are steering, coordinating, shaping, unblocking. And often you are making progress. But your brain is still paying the reload cost each time it has to re-enter a different task.

In other words, AI can reduce keystrokes without reducing cognitive fragmentation.

This is why working with agents can feel strangely tiring even when you are producing more. The work has moved up a level, but it has not disappeared. A lot of it has simply been transformed into orchestration overhead. You are now responsible for loading context, packaging intent, checking fidelity, spotting subtle misses, and deciding when something is good enough to keep moving. That is real work. In some cases, it is harder than just doing the task yourself, especially when the task is fuzzy and the failure modes are conceptual instead of mechanical.

Software engineering makes this even sharper. The deeper the abstraction, the greater the re-entry cost. If I switch away from a simple concrete task, I lose a little momentum. If I switch away from a half-formed systems problem, I may lose the entire mental model I was building. That is why shallow interruptions in engineering often feel disproportionately expensive. You are not just losing time. You are losing the invisible structure your mind was assembling.

Context engineering can accidentally industrialize that problem. Instead of one deep task, you now manage five shallow supervisory loops around five deep tasks. The system looks efficient from the outside. Internally, your attention is shredded.

I do not think the answer is to reject AI or romanticize doing everything manually. Context engineering is real leverage. Used well, it is one of the most valuable new skills in software work. But it has to be paired with protection for focus.

That means batching similar work instead of constantly hopping levels. It means giving agents fewer, larger handoffs rather than a stream of tiny interventions. It means building reusable context so you are not re-explaining the same thing all day. It means protecting blocks of time where you stay inside one problem long enough to get past the 10-minute surface layer and into actual thought.

Most importantly, it means recognizing that orchestration is not free.

The point of context engineering is not to eliminate attention cost. It is to spend attention more intentionally. If you forget that, AI does not free you from context switching. It just gives you a faster, more sophisticated way to fragment your day.

---

[^1]: Gloria Mark, *Attention Span: A Groundbreaking Way to Restore Balance, Happiness and Productivity* (Hanover Square Press, 2023). Mark’s broader research on interruptions and fragmented attention in knowledge work is the relevant point here.
[^2]: Sophie Leroy, “Why Is It So Hard to Do My Work? The Challenge of Attention Residue When Switching Between Work Tasks,” *Organization Science* 20, no. 2 (2009): 168–181.
